replies.text: No contrast, no shadow: It's basically pre-production indecision and post-production fixing to blame for this. If you don't know what you want to do, and have  lot of green screen, you have to light everything well, as evenly as possible, which means indeed: no contrast, no shadow. And then you drop everything off in post production in the hopes they can make things right. Which in general won't happen.

replies.author: @petercampi2840
replies.text: @nwoizaakgoldmann4519 1995, when 20th Century Fox released the flop known as Waterworld. It failed in domestic boxoffices, but made its money back in international showings. It turned out that big budget special FX works did very well in countries like China, where the crowds weren't big on reading subtitles, but loved seeing the spectacles. That's when the big studios learned that they didn't need to put any effort into the story or casting, just as long as the special effects were fancy enough the movie would make its money back in the East. This is the real reason they became so forgiving of atrocious works of film.

author: @TheHouseOfWaffles
text:
Ghostbusters 2016 marked a cultural change in entertainment.  The Internet Movie Database was THE BEST place on the internet to connect with other people to talk about movies, but then it was shut down and deleted as a means of silencing all the so-called "misogynists" and "trolls" who were just people who didn't worship a garbage movie.

This was the jumpstart of Hollyweird making every possible effort to discredit, dismiss, and suppress any voices of criticisim about the rubbish direction in which the industry was headed, which soon afterward included collusion with entertainment news entities.

Now, if you dislike anything at all about the latest braindead, lazy, nonsensical celluloid bucket of puke, your opinion (and wallet) don't matter because you're some kind of _ist_ or _phobe._  And it all started with this piece of refuse.

replies.text:
@theALTF4 Again, at the time, I fucking hated her for it too

Now, I'm not so sure. This was the first time something like this had really kicked off in the mainstream. We had dealt with gamergate the year before (I still contend that GamerGate was the catalyst that kicked off the "Cold" stage of the culture war) and I had already become used to pandering attempts to push "THE MESSAGE" by this point; the normies and the mainstream? Not so much - and not for a while.

This was a brand new phenomenon to these actors/directors who - in some cases - hadn't even fully reckoned with how online-centric the culture, our politics and media had become. I think peripheries used the film as a vehicle for their agenda, more than the actual film did - and were I McCarthy, dealing with a sort-of cultural backlash completely new to me: I'd be inclined to trust the studio when they say "It's a misogyny campaign."

It was trite, it was garbage, but was it intentionally subversive? I don't know. All I do know is: three SNL cast members doing Improv for an entire shoot is something I wouldn't even subject terrorists to.

replies.text: People like that don't get it. They're the same folks who defend any political movie by saying "but movie X was political and did well!" Movies can be political, they can be controversial, they can be a soapbox for the director or actors or writers, they can tackle modern issues, but they need to be a good movie first and foremost. A good movie doesn't need to be political, but a political movie does need to be good. So maybe this movie was important for feminists, maybe it said things that needed to be said, but that's irrelevant: it was a bad movie, argument over.

replies.text:
Youtube removed dislikes in 2021 in response to dislikes on the White House official channel when Brandon was not particularly at his most popular. This movie came out a full 4 years before that. I believe this movie did cause IMDb to make changes though. 

Other notable and notorious movies that caused radical changes in reviewing are: 
- The Amy Schumer special, which notoriously got a sub-1 star rating on Netflix, causing Netflix to drop their star rating system in favor of an invisible thumbs up/down system that only the algorithm saw (and likely ignored). 
- The Last Jedi, which inspired outlets like Rotten Tomatoes to cherrypick out bad reviews that were removed for apparently not including enough detail to prove the reviewer watched the movie, despite 'good' reviews being as little as one or two words and left alone. 
- Captain Marvel, which finally committed Rotten Tomatoes to fully manipulating the audience score through their 'verified' audience score system.

author: @MaesterMike
text:
What really disgusted me during the whole debacle behind this movie is when the left and Hollywood went after James Rolfe of Angry Video Game Nerd fame when he made a short video on why he wasn't going to see it. I watched it moments before this video, and his reasons had nothing to do with it being a gender swapped reboot, and moreso that the movie itself looked bad and it being named just "Ghostbusters" made it have an identity crisis. It was a perfectly innocuous video, and he explained his points in a calm and reasonable way, but seeing big name actors attack and misinterpret him for that made me sick. Needless to say, my hatred for Hollywood grew worse during this time.

And I'm glad this movie bombed hard. It makes me glad seeing the left's attempts at dividing people blow up in their face.

author: @BillPeschel
text:
This episode explains (for me) what the fuck happened. Because the year before, Paul Feig did "Spy," with Melissa McCarthy, and it's a genuinely funny movie. I've enjoyed McCarthy's other self-financed movies like "Tammy." So why the heck did this fail?

It had to have been Feig's insistence on adlibbing the movie. I suspect "Spy" had a solid script, which gave actors room to improvise lines (there's a clip on the DVD of Jason Statham improvising tough-guy lines that I thought was hilarious). 

Years later, when I finally saw the movie, I realized it wasn't a disaster, but it wasn't that good either. The film's story wasn't strong enough, the characters were not well-defined enough, to play off each other as effectively as in "Spy."

And, of course, this is the first movie I'm aware of that the creators decided to try the "let's call all the haters racists and see what happens" tactic. That they haven't learned a decade later to keep their mouths shut tells me how bad Hollywood has fallen. Louie B. Mayer would weep.

This has been my TEDx talk.

author: @nicosmind3
text: Despite "the backlash" I tried to give the movie a chance (pirate streaming I wasn't paying) and it's probably the controversy that pushed me to give it a chance. I wanted to see for myself even though I thought it looked as funny as an Adam Sandler movie, it seemed to have that exact same Sandler humour. And for me the comedy wasn't the worst aspect of it, it was that the main cast were so unlikable. I couldn't understand having 4 people who were insufferable and hard to watch, and gave up just after an hour. I don't think I've heard anyone else talk about having insufferable characters, but if even one of them passed for normal I'd probably have latched onto her and finished the movie. If we can't cheer on the protagonist at least give us time with the antagonist so we can cheer them on instead. I didn't even mind the bad comedy even though I assumed I would hate it, I just thought one hour with our four "heroes" was too much for me to bare!!!

When they are characters with a low likeability baseline floating out in generic fantasy plot world, it's like having 3 White Witches on screen at the same time (in this analogy Leslie Jones gets to play the flunky coachman). Kate McKinnon's entire role was to sit there smirking at things. What WAS she supposed to do with that?
